full_text:
- Title (limit of 165 characters including spaces) - **Single-cell
 > spatial atlas of high-grade serous ovarian cancer uncovers MHC
 > class II as a key predictor of spatial tumor ecosystems and
 > clinical outcomes** (152 characters)

- Running title (limit of 60 characters including spaces) **MHCII
 > predicts spatial immune activation in HGSC (62/60 characters)**

 - **MHCII predicts immune activation and clinical outcomes**

- Unstructured Abstract

The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant--host cell
interactions, yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian
carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive
single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients,
integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Across 929
single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically
heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell
co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts
clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we identify tumor cell MHCII
expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. Validation
with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic
datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with
local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR
clonality.

distinct spatial transcriptomic
datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with
local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR
clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show
that tumor MHCII expression correlates with increased CD8+ T cell
cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers novel
insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient
stratification in HGSC.

**(149/150 words)**

- Statement of significance (a one- or two-sentence summary of the
 > manuscript's salient findings)

We present a multi-omics, spatially resolved atlas of the TME in
metastatic HGSC, profiling 15.1 million cells to identify spatial
domains linked to clinicomolecular subtypes and clinical outcomes. Using
our pipeline CEFIIRA, we uncover key features such as tumor-intrinsic
MHC-II expression, a driver of prolonged survival that mediates immune
infiltration, antigen presentation, and immunotherapy responses in HGSC.
